full_response:
Step–by–step reasoning

1. Definition of a global inertial frame  
   • In special relativity a global inertial frame is a single coordinate system that covers all of space-time and in which the metric is the Minkowski metric ηab everywhere:  
     ds² = –c²dt² + dx² + dy² + dz²,  
     so all Christoffel symbols Γa​bc = 0 everywhere.  
   • World-lines of free particles are straight lines (zero 4-acceleration) in such a frame.

2. What gravity looks like experimentally  
   • Locally (inside a small elevator) one can always choose coordinates in which a freely-falling observer is momentarily inertial; this is the equivalence principle.  
   • Globally, however, one measures tidal effects: two neighboring freely-falling particles separate according to the geodesic-deviation equation  
     d²ξa/dτ² = –Rab​cd Ub Uc ξd,  
     where Rab​cd ≠ 0 in a gravitational field.  
     Tidal accelerations cannot be removed by any single choice of coordinates.

3. Mathematical statement  
   • A necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of a global inertial frame is vanishing Riemann curvature Rab​cd = 0 everywhere, because then one can find a coordinate transformation that makes Γa​bc = 0 globally.  
   • In the presence of mass–energy, Einstein’s equation  
     Gab = 8πGN Tab  
     generally gives Rab​cd ≠ 0.  
   • Therefore no coordinate system exists in which the metric is everywhere Minkowskian; at best one can make it flat in an infinitesimal neighborhood of a point.

4. Physical interpretation  
   • Gravity is not a force that acts within flat space-time; it is encoded in the curvature of space-time itself.  
   • Because curvature is an intrinsic property, it cannot be eliminated by any global change of coordinates—only by passing to many small locally inertial patches (normal coordinates).

Conclusion  
Global inertial frames fail to account for gravity because gravitational fields correspond to space-time curvature, and curvature prevents the existence of a single coordinate system in which free particles move along straight lines everywhere. One needs a manifold covered by many local inertial frames, i.e. the framework of general relativity.

\boxed{\text{Because gravity curves space–time, no single (global) inertial frame in which the metric is everywhere Minkowski can exist; tidal effects remain in any large region, so only local inertial frames are possible.}}

Confidence: 87%
